Wow, I ran a few scenarios against `ffmpeg` using `drawtext`, and `ffmpeg`'s string unescaping is super bizarre. Some chars only need a single backslash before them, some require two backslashes, and some require _eight backslashes_.

Here's an example that draws the string `a\b`:
```
ffmpeg -i ffmpeg/tests/sample_data/dummy.mp4 -filter_complex '[0]drawtext=x=0:y=100:text=a\\\\\\\\b:fontfile=/Library/Fonts/Arial.ttf[1]' -map '[1]' out.mp4
```
Yup, _eight backslashes_.

I found [this stackoverflow article](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/10725225/ffmpeg-single-quote-in-drawtext) talking about it:
> Special character escapes are like violence: if they're not solving your problem, you're not using enough.
> `ffmpeg -i test.mpg -vf drawtext=text="It\\\\\'s so easy"`

I'm gonna try to see if there's any method to the madness and whether there's a way to completely correctly escape. It's nice to have this done in a tool like `ffmpeg-python` so that hopefully nobody else has to deal with ffmpeg's insanity.

Here's what I presume. For local inhibition, the `inhibitionRadius_` isn't really a parameter, it changes with the statistics of the data. So if you're using the `numActiveColumnsPerInhArea_` parameter, you can accidentally wind up in situations where you're activating way more columns than you want, because the inhibition areas are small.

This does not apply to global inhibition unless someone goes in and manually changes the inhibition radius. `::initialize` will set this radius to cover the whole space. So in this code, the inhibition radius is predictable. So I don't think we should perform `min` check here.

Similarly, I would argue that this logic in `inhibitColumns_` should be moved to `inhibitColumnsLocal_`, and `inhibitColumnsGlobal_` should just obey the parameters. Though maybe that's out of scope for this change.

Ultimately this is all a hack that exists because the `numActiveColumnsPerInhArea` parameter is awkward when mixed with local inhibition. With local inhibition it's probably best to use the `localAreaDensity` parameter instead.

I did this originally.  But, after seeing how the code was coming together and thinking of the models a bit, I came to the conclusion that the bucket was a better place for this.  

Here is my reasoning; I'd love your feedback:

1) The blob object doesn't facilitate querying, rather it's a representation of what the storage system believes exists.  Allowing someone to define the generation of a blob, through a setter, seems to be counter to the purpose of the generation property on the blob.  That is, blob.generation is the canonical ID returned by the storage system for that blob.  Not something a user should be able to define.  

2) The blob.path is an imputed property and constructed from the only two key pieces of information needed to define a blob: bucket and name.  User-defined generation seems a bit superfluous, since it has no real meaning in the definition of a blob.

These two points led me to think of the generation as a query parameter better suited to the bucket API.

The problem with my approach now is that it disregards all other query parameters (ifGenerationNotMatch, ifMetagenerationNotMatch, etc.).  

I think the next step for this path of development would be a generator function, in the bucket, that uses user-specified query parameters and the blob.path to create the full API query URL.

Let me know your thoughts... if you would like the query param to be through a setter to be at the blob level, and corresponding blob.path construction to happen there, I can refactor.

```python
return response.render_template('my_template.html', context={'things': things})
```

```
hashcat -m 10 90be209e598d48fbd6a5e144a7a9ebf0:ebddd18303a54111e2dea05a92ab46b4 --hex-charset --hex-salt -a 3 --increment '00?l?l?l?l?l?l?l?l'
```

As for the "out the window", there is a bit of subtlety here:
- for things like the automatic preset workflows, the spirit is more like what you said - i.e., just do whatever recipe you think is best. If that means half_kpts, so be it - these are details the user doesn't necessarily want to worry about.
- however, the original intention of RunVaspCustodian (the Firework object) was to be used as a drop-in replacement for RunVaspDirect. If one used the latter, VASP would be directly called. If you did a drop-in replacement of RunVaspDirect->RunVaspCustodian, the user expectation (from my point of view) is that everything should behave essentially the same as running VASP directly except you would get error correction for free. However this only really pertains to job_type="normal" - my interpretation of that is "VASP with error correction". For job types other than "normal" (e.g. NEB), this drop-in replacement thing is indeed out the window and one is free to do whatever since those not-normal job types are like mini workflows.

The ReST structure here is:
```
Python API
 |- Tracing
 |   |- Classes
 |   |   |- opentracing.Tracer
 |   |   |- opentracing.ReferenceType
 |   |   `- opentracing.Reference
 |   `- Utility Functions
 |       |- opentracing.start_child_span
 |       |- opentracing.child_of
 |       `- opentracing.follows_from
 |- Spans
 |   |- opentracing.Span
 |   `- opentracing.SpanContext
 |- Propagation
 |   `- opentracing.Format
 `- Exceptions
     |- opentracing.InvalidCarrierException
     |- opentracing.SpanContextCorruptedException
```

@nikhilk We also need the query body to go in the cell. One solution I see is using yaml syntax like we do in other magics, something like:
```
%%bq query --name myquery
- query: SELECT * FROM `mydataset.mytable WHERE field1 = @param1 AND field2 >= @param2
```
